note_data.title: Coordination
keyPoints: All living organisms are made of cells that use ATP from respiration for energy-requiring processes
Eukaryotic cells contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells do not contain membrane-bound organelles
The nucleus is surrounded by a double membrane called the nuclear envelope containing pores, and contains chromatin and a nucleolus
Mitochondria are oval-shaped with a double membrane envelope, inner membrane folded into cristae, and contain the matrix with enzymes for respiration
Plant cells uniquely contain chloroplasts (site of photosynthesis), vacuoles, cell walls made of cellulose, and plasmodesmata
Animal cells may contain cilia (hair-like structures in '9+2' formation) and microvilli (finger-like projections that increase surface area)
Prokaryotic cells are unicellular, 1-5μm in diameter, with circular DNA and smaller 70S ribosomes compared to eukaryotic 80S ribosomes
Viruses are non-living structures consisting of nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein capsid, sometimes with an envelope

definitions.definition: A more detailed structure of cells that can be obtained by using a microscope
Projections formed by the folded inner membrane of mitochondria
A protective protein coat that encloses the nucleic acid in viruses
Small channels that pass through the cell wall of adjoining plant cells to allow communication between cells
The membrane that surrounds the vacuole in plant cells

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events in logical order with linking words

modelAnswer.question: Explain how an action potential is transmitted along an unmyelinated axon. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Stimulus causes depolarisation of axon membrane. When threshold potential (-55mV) is reached, voltage-gated sodium channels open. Sodium ions rush into axon down concentration gradient making inside positive (+40mV). Local currents spread to adjacent regions of membrane causing depolarisation to spread. Voltage-gated potassium channels open and potassium ions leave axon. Sodium-potassium pump actively transports 3Na+ out and 2K+ in to restore resting potential (-70mV).
markBreakdown: Mark 6: Sodium-potassium pump restores resting potential

commonMistakes: Confusing the structure of prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes - prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes while eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes in cytoplasm
Forgetting that mitochondria and chloroplasts contain their own small circular DNA and 70S ribosomes
Mixing up organelles that are exclusive to plant cells versus animal cells
Incorrectly stating that viruses are living organisms when they are non-living structures
examinerTips: Remember the specific membrane arrangements - nucleus has double membrane with pores, mitochondria have double membrane with inner folded into cristae
Learn the unique features of each cell type: prokaryotes have peptidoglycan cell walls and are much smaller (1-5μm) than eukaryotic cells
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Action potential graph showing resting potential (-70mV), threshold (-55mV), depolarisation peak (+40mV), repolarisation and refractory period over time. Label voltage-gated Na+ and K+ channel opening/closing points.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how the nervous system coordinates a reflex action when touching a hot object
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of which part of the brain controls balance and coordination
M/J 2022 P2 Q6(c) (4 marks) — required students to compare nervous and hormonal coordination in terms of speed and duration of response
O/N 2021 P2 Q3(b) (5 marks) — asked for a detailed explanation of how insulin coordinates blood glucose regulation including feedback mechanisms

workedExample.question: Explain how the body coordinates the response when blood glucose levels rise after eating a meal. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the stimulus - blood glucose concentration rises above normal levels after eating
Step 2: Identify the receptor - specialized cells in the pancreas (beta cells in islets of Langerhans) detect the high glucose
Step 3: Explain hormone release - pancreas releases insulin into the bloodstream
Step 4: Describe target organs - insulin travels to liver and muscle cells (effectors)
Step 5: Explain the response - insulin causes glucose uptake by cells and conversion to glycogen for storage
Step 6: State the result - blood glucose returns to normal levels (negative feedback)
workedExample.answer: When blood glucose rises, receptor cells in the pancreas detect this change. The pancreas releases insulin hormone into the blood. Insulin travels to target organs (liver and muscles) where it stimulates glucose uptake and conversion to glycogen. This lowers blood glucose back to normal levels through negative feedback control.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: stimulus identified, receptor named, hormone named, target organ mentioned, response described, negative feedback concept

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the nervous system with the endocrine system' — always specify whether coordination is by nerves (electrical, fast) or hormones (chemical, slower)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often missed the feedback mechanism' — always mention negative feedback when explaining hormonal coordination of glucose, temperature, or water balance

Examiner Report 2021: 'Vague answers about 'the brain controls everything'' — be specific about which part coordinates what (cerebellum for balance, medulla for breathing, hypothalamus for temperature)
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a reflex arc showing: stimulus arrow pointing to receptor in skin, sensory neuron leading to spinal cord, synapse in spinal cord connecting to motor neuron, motor neuron leading to effector muscle, and response arrow. Include labels for all parts and show direction of nerve impulse with arrows. Gray matter should be shown in center of spinal cord cross-section.
note_data.quickRecap: Cells are the basic units of all living organisms and use ATP for energy processes. Eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles including nucleus, mitochondria, and ER, while prokaryotic cells lack these structures and have circular DNA. Plant cells have additional organelles like chloroplasts and vacuoles, while animal cells may have cilia and microvilli. Viruses are non-living structures with nucleic acid in a protein capsid.
